title: Beef Wellington
subtitle: Beef fillet, mushroom duxelles, wrapped in puff pastry
excerpt: Beef fillet wrapped in a layer of mushroom duxelles and Parma ham, then enclosed in puff pastry and baked until the pastry is golden and the beef is medium-rare. The combination of textures makes it the most technically demanding of the British celebration roasts, but not beyond a careful home cook.
sourceNotes: Beef Wellington is associated with the Duke of Wellington, though the historical connection is not well documented. The dish appeared in British and American cookbooks in the 20th century and reached peak popularity in the 1970s and 1980s as a dinner party centrepiece. Gordon Ramsay popularised a refined version in the 2000s that became the standard reference for the modern recipe.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and chill up to 24 hours ahead, wrapped tightly in cling film. Score and egg wash just before baking.

content.text: The duxelles must be cooked completely dry. Mushrooms contain a great deal of water; if the duxelles is still wet when you assemble the Wellington, it will steam the pastry from the inside and prevent it crisping. Cook until the pan is completely dry and the mushrooms are a dark, compact paste.

Use a meat thermometer. The fillet is seared before assembly but is raw inside; the oven time is short. Pull it at 50°C for medium-rare; it will rise to 55°C during the 10-minute rest. The pastry gives a visual cue (golden brown) but is not a reliable indicator of the meat's internal temperature.

items.prepNote: chestnut or button, blitzed to a paste

content.text: Blitz the {{mushrooms-chestnut}} to a coarse paste in a food processor. Cook in a dry frying pan over high heat, stirring constantly, for 10 to 12 minutes until all the moisture has evaporated and the paste is dry and dark. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Cool completely.

Season the {{beef-fillet}} all over with salt and pepper. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a heavy pan until smoking. Sear the fillet on all sides for 2 minutes total — just enough to colour the outside. Cool completely, then brush all over with {{dijon-mustard}}.

Lay a large sheet of cling film on the work surface. Arrange the {{prosciutto}} slices overlapping in a rectangle slightly longer than the fillet. Spread the cooled duxelles over the ham in an even layer. Place the mustard-coated fillet at the near edge and roll tightly using the cling film to form a cylinder. Twist the ends to tighten. Chill for at least 30 minutes. Roll out the {{puff-pastry}} on a lightly floured surface to a rectangle large enough to enclose the fillet. Remove the cling film from the fillet and place at the edge of the pastry. Roll up, pressing to seal. Trim the excess, fold the ends under, and press to seal. Brush all over with beaten {{eggs}}. Score the surface lightly with a knife. Chill for at least 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C. Place the Wellington on a baking tray. Brush with a second coat of egg wash. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes, until the pastry is deep golden brown. Check the internal temperature: remove at 50°C for medium-rare. Rest on a board for 10 minutes before slicing with a sharp serrated knife.

items.cause: Duxelles not cooked dry enough, or Wellington not chilled before baking
items.fix: Cook the duxelles until completely dry. The chilling step is essential — a warm assembly causes the butter in the pastry to melt before it reaches the oven, producing a greasy bottom

items.symptom: Overcooked beef with perfectly golden pastry
items.cause: Oven too hot, or Wellington too thick
items.fix: Use a thermometer and check at 20 minutes. A thicker Wellington needs lower heat (200°C) and more time

items.symptom: Pastry tears when rolling the log
items.cause: Pastry too warm or rolled too thin
items.fix: Work quickly and chill the pastry before rolling if it has become warm. The pastry needs to be 3 to 4 mm thick to hold the filling without tearing

content.text: Beef Wellington is the special occasion roast for households who want to cook something more technically ambitious than a standard joint. It is a dinner party dish as much as a family Sunday lunch, and its visual impact when sliced at the table is part of what makes it worth the preparation. The make-ahead assembly makes it manageable for a host who wants to spend time with their guests rather than the oven.
